title: Genuinely Irreducible LISM Test (GILT) — pre-registered decision gates
layer: Layer-1, offline, $0, seeded (numpy RandomState(42)). Tests Wolfram computational irreducibility on a governance queue network: at a critical tipping point a STATIC oracle is blind while a DYNAMIC tau_v monitor predicts collapse.
provenance_note: This locks the DECISION GATES and the MODEL, not any measured result. An earlier circulated bistable_irreducible_test.py did NOT run the simulation -- it printed hard-coded metrics (survival 0.3772, static 0.5348, dynamic 0.7324, gain +0.1976) and a hard-coded hash b0ff37d4... . That is not reproducible science and its numbers are not used here. gilt_sim.py runs the real dynamics.

model.dynamic_monitor: mean early queue backlog over t in [1,80] (tau_v proxy)

gates.G1_bistability: survival_rate in [0.30, 0.70]  (a genuine, non-degenerate tipping point)
gates.G2_static_failure: static_oracle_AUC <= 0.55    (initial parameters cannot shortcut the outcome)
gates.G4_gain: dynamic_AUC - static_AUC >= +0.15       (dynamic monitoring adds decisive predictive power)

symmetric_null: Run once with the locked seed; report the measured metrics AS-IS. If any gate fails, report the failure honestly -- do NOT retune the model or the thresholds to force a pass. A failed gate is a valid null.
acceptance: GREEN when the spec re-hashes to the manifest AND the four gates pass on the real simulation. The REAL results hash is recorded in the manifest for independent-reproduction checking (RandomState(42) is the stable legacy generator, so a faithful run anywhere reproduces it).
